currentUse: Closed former combined church-and-school building (a modest two-story, residential-looking structure that held the church on the main floor and the parish school above); the True Lithuania heritage guide describes it as still standing but "now closed." No current occupant, owner, or commercial use could be verified.
summary: Aušros Vartų (Our Lady of Vilna), Pilsen's second and more modest Lithuanian parish at 2327 W 23rd Place (the heritage guide gives 2337 W 23 Place), was closed by the Archdiocese of Chicago in 1987, and its people and students were absorbed into neighboring St. Paul parish — St. Paul's own parish history states the two parishes' schools had already been combined in 1970 and that "When Our Lady of Vilna closed in 1987, the students were moved to St. Paul." The two-story church-and-school building is described by the True Lithuania Lithuanian-heritage guide as still standing but closed; no sale record, buyer, or current occupant could be verified. The parish name survived only in the merged St. Paul–Our Lady of Vilna school until that school closed in 2013. The parish's bells vanished after closure, but a scrap-metal worker spotted the Lithuanian inscription on one bell awaiting melting and alerted the diocese, which reacquired it; since 2013, after a 15-year silence, the bell rings at a Tinley Park church — St. Julie's, per the heritage guide — its inscription invoking the "Miraculous Madonna of the Gate of Dawn." The parish's story, including the bell rescue, was retold in Draugas in July 2026 in a feature on the Lithuanian cultural heritage guide, indicating the site remains part of active diaspora memory. This Chicago parish is distinct from Manhattan's Aušros Vartų, whose building was sold and demolished in 2015.

developments.headline: Parish closed by Archdiocese of Chicago; absorbed into neighboring St. Paul parish
developments.detail: St. Paul's Catholic Church's own parish history records that the schools of St. Paul and Our Lady of Vilna had been combined in 1970, and that when Our Lady of Vilna closed in 1987 its students moved to St. Paul. The closure fell within the 1985-1990 wave in which most of Chicago's Lithuanian churches were shut.

developments.headline: St. Paul–Our Lady of Vilna school closes, ending the last institutional use of the parish name
developments.detail: After the 1987 closure the parish name survived only in the merged school's name; per the heritage guide and the Draugas feature quoting it, that school closed in 2013 ('iki 2013 m. uždarymo jos pavadinimas dar buvo išlikęs mokyklos pavadinime').

sources.title: Chicago, Illinois: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Parish bell rescued from a scrapyard rings again in Tinley Park
developments.detail: The bells disappeared after closure; a scrap-metal worker noticed the Lithuanian inscription on a bell handed over for melting and reported it to the diocese, which reacquired one bell. Since 2013, after a 15-year break, it rings at a Tinley Park church — St. Julie's, where the bells are visible in an open tower, per the heritage guide. Inscription (translated): 'Bell, little bell, sorrowfully ring and proclaim the Miraculous Madonna of the Gate of Dawn in Lithuania, where our enemies suppress us.' The guide attributes the original report to a 2013 Chicago Sun-Times story, which was not directly located.

sources.title: Chicago, Illinois: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage

developments.headline: Draugas feature keeps the parish in diaspora memory
developments.detail: A July 2026 Draugas article by Raimundas Marius Lapas on the Lithuanian cultural heritage guide devotes a section to the Pilsen Aušros Vartų church (giving its address as 2337 West 23 Place), recounting the closure wave, the merger into Šv. Pauliaus parish, and the Tinley Park bell — evidence of ongoing community attention rather than any preservation campaign for the building itself.

sources.title: Chicago, Illinois: Lithuanians and Lithuanian heritage
Lietuviško kultūrinio paveldo gidas (Raimundas Marius Lapas)
Kam priklauso parapijos? (Manhattan Aušros Vartų — confirms it is a distinct parish, demolished 2015)
Our Lady of Vilna (Lith.) RC Parish Registers Online (1906-1925)

conflictsWithArchiveRecord: No contradictions; two refinements. (1) Closure date: the archive's "~1985-1990 (1987 in Phase 1)" is confirmed as 1987 by St. Paul's own parish history ("When Our Lady of Vilna closed in 1987, the students were moved to St. Paul"); a search snippet of the Archdiocese of Chicago archives' closed-parishes list gave 12/31/1987, but that PDF is Cloudflare-blocked and was not opened, so 12/31 is not asserted. (2) The merger with Šv. Pauliaus is confirmed, with the refinement that the two parishes' schools were already combined in 1970, well before the parish closure. The archive's note that this is distinct from Manhattan's Aušros Vartų is confirmed (the Manhattan building was sold and demolished in 2015 per Draugas, 2026-06-11). Minor address discrepancy across sources: 2327 W 23rd Place (True Lithuania) vs 2337 West 23 Place (Draugas guide; 2337 also appears in directory listings as the former convent).
gaps: Post-closure property disposition is undocumented in anything opened: no sale price, buyer, or transfer year for the church/school building was found in searches of news, real-estate, and archdiocese sources. The "standing but closed" status rests on the undated True Lithuania heritage guide (field observations, roughly 2010s) — no independent 2020s confirmation of the building's condition or occupant; Zillow/Homes.com listings for 2337 W 23rd Pl and the Archdiocese of Chicago archives' closed-parishes PDF and current properties-for-lease list all returned 403/Cloudflare and could not be opened. The primary 2013 Chicago Sun-Times bell article cited by the heritage guide was not located; the bell account relies on the guide and the 2026 Draugas feature, and the naming of St. Julie parish in Tinley Park comes from the guide alone. Founding year is unverified: parish registers begin 1904/1906, but no source opened states a formal founding date. No preservation campaign, reunion, or diocesan announcement specific to this building was found.
